Question: Episodes of severe burning pain in the hands and feet, present since childhood, in this disease are most characteristically triggered by:
Option A: Heat, fever, and physical exertion
Option B: Cold exposure, stress, and certain foods
Option C: Sustained pressure on the affected limbs
Option D: Prolonged immobility, such as long flights or bed rest
Notes: Naive guess: cold (Raynaud's / diabetic neuropathy paradigm).

Question: A 32-year-old man with this disease is asking about inheritance. Which family-history pattern would most support the diagnosis?
Option A: Both parents unaffected, with approximately 25% of siblings affected regardless of sex
Option B: Affected maternal uncles and male cousins, with no father-to-son transmission
Option C: Affected men in multiple generations on the father’s side, including father-to-son transmission
Option D: No family history of similar symptoms in any relative
Notes: X-linked. Naive guess: autosomal dominant (option C).

Question: A patient with this disease has a long-standing diagnosis of irritable bowel syndrome with episodic abdominal pain and diarrhea. This gastrointestinal pattern is most likely:
Option A: A coincidental functional bowel disorder unrelated to the underlying disease
Option B: An adverse effect of the chronic neuropathic-pain medications he takes
Option C: A direct gastrointestinal manifestation of the disease, often predating its diagnosis
Option D: A sign of mesenteric ischemia from vascular involvement, warranting urgent mesenteric angiography
Notes: GI is part of the disease, not separate.

Question: On physical examination, which of the following dermatologic findings would most strongly support this diagnosis?
Option A: Erythematous, silvery-scaled plaques on the extensor surfaces of the elbows and knees
Option B: Clusters of small, dark-red papules over the lower abdomen, groin, and upper thighs
Option C: Hyperpigmented, velvety thickened patches in the axillae and the posterior neck folds
Option D: Firm yellow-orange nodules overlying the Achilles and the extensor tendons of the hands
Notes: Angiokeratomas in 'swimming-trunk' distribution. Distractors: psoriasis, acanthosis nigricans, xanthomas.

Question: Cornea verticillata (bilateral whorl-like opacities of the corneal epithelium) is observed on slit-lamp examination. Which of the following is true about this finding?
Option A: It is pathognomonic for this disease and reliably excludes all other diagnoses
Option B: It typically causes progressive visual loss that requires corneal transplantation
Option C: It also occurs with chronic amiodarone, chloroquine, or hydroxychloroquine therapy
Option D: It is usually markedly asymmetric, affecting only one eye in the majority of patients
Notes: Strong AI-vs-Control differentiator. UpToDate explicitly lists drug-induced cornea verticillata.

Question: Sensory examination of a patient with this disease typically shows:
Option A: Reduced pinprick and temperature sensation distally, with preserved vibration sense
Option B: Reduced vibration and proprioception in the feet, with preserved pinprick and temperature
Option C: A patchy, asymmetric sensory deficit consistent with a mononeuritis multiplex pattern
Option D: A symmetric 'stocking-glove' loss of all sensory modalities with absent ankle reflexes
Notes: Small-fiber selective. Naive guess: diabetic stocking-glove (D).

Question: For a male patient with clinical suspicion of this disease, the most appropriate initial confirmatory test is:
Option A: Renal biopsy with electron microscopy looking for lamellated inclusion bodies
Option B: Whole-exome sequencing performed on a peripheral blood sample
Option C: Serum protein electrophoresis with immunofixation
Option D: Plasma or leukocyte alpha-galactosidase A enzyme activity assay
Notes: Distractor C is the amyloid trap.

Question: The patient’s 35-year-old sister is being evaluated as a possible carrier. Her plasma alpha-galactosidase A activity is reported as normal. The most appropriate interpretation is:
Option A: Send GLA gene sequencing, because enzyme activity is unreliable in carrier females
Option B: She is definitively unaffected and requires no further evaluation or genetic testing
Option C: Her diagnosis is confirmed; the normal enzyme activity reflects compensatory pathways
Option D: She has a 50% chance of carrying it and should defer testing until she is symptomatic
Notes: Strongest differentiator. X-inactivation in heterozygous females. UpToDate emphasizes; AI may gloss over.

Question: Which of the following ECG findings is characteristic of cardiac involvement in this disease, particularly in earlier stages?
Option A: A prolonged QTc interval with marked T-wave inversion
Option B: Right bundle branch block with marked right axis deviation
Option C: Pathologic Q waves in the inferior and lateral leads
Option D: A short PR interval, often less than 120 milliseconds
Notes: Strong differentiator. Case vignette ECG actually shows PR 110 ms; Control reading carefully notices.

Question: Migalastat (Galafold) for this disease:
Option A: Is an oral substrate reduction therapy that inhibits the enzyme glucosylceramide synthase
Option B: Is a pharmacologic chaperone that works only in patients with 'amenable' GLA mutations
Option C: Is given intravenously every two weeks alongside standard enzyme replacement therapy
Option D: Is curative when it is combined with hematopoietic stem-cell transplantation early on
Notes: Strong differentiator. Distractor A is Gaucher (eliglustat). Migalastat is genotype-restricted.

Question: In a patient with this disease, stage 3 CKD, and persistent proteinuria, a cornerstone of renal protection — independent of disease-specific therapy — is:
Option A: A high-dose loop diuretic such as furosemide to control chronic fluid overload
Option B: Chronic empiric oral corticosteroids to slow progressive glomerular injury over time
Option C: An ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker titrated to reduce proteinuria
Option D: A calcineurin inhibitor such as tacrolimus to suppress ongoing glomerular inflammation
Notes: General nephrology + Fabry-specific recommendation.

Question: You are counseling a male patient with this disease about implications for his future children. Which of the following statements is most accurate?
Option A: Each of his children, regardless of sex, has a 50% chance of inheriting the mutation
Option B: Only his sons are at risk; his daughters cannot inherit the mutation
Option C: None of his children are at increased risk because the disease typically arises de novo
Option D: All of his daughters will inherit the mutation; none of his sons will inherit it
Notes: Hemizygous male: all daughters inherit the X, no sons. Naive trap: autosomal dominant 50/50.

Question: The neuropathic pain experienced in Fabry disease is most directly related to:
Option A: Deposition of glycosphingolipids within small nerve fibers
Option B: Immune-mediated demyelination of the peripheral sensory nerves
Option C: Entrapment and compression neuropathy from soft-tissue edema
Option D: Autoimmune small-vessel vasculitis affecting the peripheral nerves
Notes: Small-fiber deposition; distractors are other neuropathy mechanisms.

Question: Which mechanism most likely explains the development of left ventricular hypertrophy in Fabry disease?
Option A: Chronic, poorly controlled systemic arterial hypertension
Option B: Coronary vasospasm causing recurrent ischemia and fibrosis
Option C: Recurrent viral myocarditis with inflammatory remodeling
Option D: Glycosphingolipid accumulation within the cardiomyocytes
Notes: Storage cardiomyopathy, not pressure-overload LVH.

Question: Proteinuria in Fabry disease most directly reflects injury to which renal structure?
Option A: Distal tubules
Option B: Collecting ducts
Option C: Renal arteries
Option D: Podocytes
Notes: Podocyte injury drives proteinuria.

Question: The primary rationale for initiating disease-specific therapy early in Fabry disease is to:
Option A: Reverse all of the organ damage that has already accumulated
Option B: Eliminate the risk of transmitting the disease to offspring
Option C: Prevent irreversible organ fibrosis and loss of function
Option D: Restore a normal life expectancy in essentially all patients
Notes: Therapy halts progression; cannot reverse established fibrosis.

Question: Which patient presentation should most strongly prompt consideration of Fabry disease?
Option A: Young man with proteinuric CKD, lifelong burning pain in the hands and feet, and a maternal uncle on dialysis
Option B: Young woman with proteinuria, a photosensitive facial rash, and inflammatory polyarthralgia of the hands
Option C: Young man with persistent hematuria, progressive sensorineural hearing loss, and a family history of kidney failure
Option D: Young adult with episodic flank pain, gross hematuria, and numerous bilateral renal cysts seen on imaging
Notes: Multisystem young-patient pattern.

Question: Beyond left ventricular hypertrophy, which of the following is another characteristic cardiac manifestation of Fabry disease?
Option A: Recurrent paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia arising from a congenital accessory pathway
Option B: Progressive conduction disease and arrhythmias, such as AV block and atrial fibrillation
Option C: Congenital long-QT syndrome that predisposes the patient to torsades de pointes
Option D: Pulmonary arterial hypertension that leads to progressive right heart failure
Notes: Conduction disease drives device therapy. (Added 2026-05-29.)

Question: In a patient on enzyme replacement therapy for Fabry disease, which blood test best reflects biochemical disease activity and treatment response over time?
Option A: Plasma globotriaosylceramide (Gb3) concentration
Option B: Plasma globotriaosylsphingosine (lyso-Gb3)
Option C: Leukocyte alpha-galactosidase A enzyme activity
Option D: A 24-hour urine protein-to-creatinine ratio
Notes: lyso-Gb3 tracks disease activity/response. (Added 2026-05-29.)

Question: Patients with Fabry disease are at markedly increased risk of stroke, often at a young age. Which stroke pattern is most characteristic?
Option A: Large-artery cardioembolic stroke as the single predominant mechanism
Option B: Lobar intracerebral hemorrhage caused by underlying cerebral amyloid angiopathy
Option C: Small-vessel ischemic stroke with a predilection for the posterior circulation
Option D: Subarachnoid hemorrhage from the rupture of a berry (saccular) aneurysm in the circle of Willis
Notes: Posterior-circulation small-vessel disease. (Added 2026-05-29.)

Question: A young man with Fabry disease reports that he cannot tolerate hot weather or exercise and rarely sweats. The most likely explanation is:
Option A: An anticholinergic adverse effect of one of his current medications
Option B: Primary hypothalamic thermoregulatory dysfunction of central origin
Option C: Physical deconditioning and reduced cardiac output from his cardiomyopathy
Option D: Fabry-related small-fiber and autonomic dysfunction that impairs sweating
Notes: Hypohidrosis from small-fiber/autonomic involvement. (Added 2026-05-29.)

Question: A patient with Fabry disease reaches end-stage kidney disease and receives a kidney transplant. Which statement is most accurate?
Option A: The graft is largely spared, but cardiac and cerebrovascular disease keep progressing
Option B: The graft re-accumulates glycosphingolipid and predictably fails within the first year
Option C: Transplantation corrects the enzyme deficiency systemically and halts disease elsewhere
Option D: A living related-donor graft is strongly preferred because relatives resist recurrence
Notes: Graft spared; systemic disease continues. (Added 2026-05-29.)

Question: How comfortable would you be taking care of a patient with Fabry disease?
Option A: 0–10 scale (0 = Not at all comfortable)
Option D: 10 = Very comfortable
Notes: Same wording is used as the intake comfort item. Paired pre/post measure.
